era_context: The year 1814 represents the climax and resolution of the Napoleonic Wars, which had dominated European politics and warfare since 1803. The Sixth Coalition — Britain, Russia, Prussia, Austria, and allied states — invaded France itself, forcing Napoleon's abdication and first exile. The resulting diplomatic settlement began reshaping the European order at the Congress of Vienna. Simultaneously, the War of 1812 between the United States and Britain reached its final phase, including the burning of Washington, D.C. In South America, independence movements against Spanish colonial rule were at a critical juncture, with royalist counteroffensives temporarily reversing patriot gains in Venezuela and Chile. In South and Southeast Asia, British imperial power continued to expand at the expense of local polities.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa beyond coastal zones and the Cape Colony
Inner Central Asia and the Kazakh steppe
Pacific Islands beyond European contact zones
Much of mainland Southeast Asia beyond major court chronicles

events.title: Treaty of Kiel — Denmark cedes Norway to Sweden
events.description: On 14 January, Denmark-Norway, which had allied with Napoleon, was forced to sign the Treaty of Kiel with Sweden and Britain. Denmark ceded Norway to the King of Sweden, ending a union between Denmark and Norway that had lasted since 1380. The treaty also transferred Swedish Pomerania to Denmark as partial compensation, and Heligoland was confirmed as a British possession. The Norwegian reaction was immediate rejection — Norwegian elites refused to accept the transfer, setting the stage for a constitutional convention and brief independence movement later that year.
key_figures: Carl Johan / Jean-Baptiste Bernadotte (Swedish Crown Prince)

sources.name: Treaty of Kiel, 14 January 1814 (treaty text)
H. Arnold Barton, Sweden and Visions of Norway: Politics and Culture 1814–1905 (2003)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text survives and is well-documented in diplomatic archives of all parties.

events.title: Norwegian Constitutional Convention at Eidsvoll
events.description: Rejecting the Treaty of Kiel, Norwegian notables convened a constitutional assembly at Eidsvoll on 10 April. Over five weeks, 112 delegates drafted one of the most liberal constitutions in Europe, heavily influenced by the American and French constitutions and the Spanish Constitution of 1812. On 17 May 1814, the assembly adopted the constitution and elected the Danish Prince Christian Frederick as King of Norway. Although Norway was forced into union with Sweden by military pressure later that year, the Eidsvoll Constitution survived largely intact and 17 May became Norway's national day. The constitution established a unicameral parliament (Storting), separation of powers, and individual liberties.
key_figures: Christian Magnus Falsen (key drafter, 'Father of the Constitution')

sources.name: Proceedings of the Eidsvoll Constitutional Assembly, 1814 (Riksforsamlingens Forhandlinger)
Karen Gammelgaard and Eirik Holmøyvik, eds., Writing Democracy: The Norwegian Constitution 1814–2014 (2014)

events.certainty_note: Assembly proceedings, delegate lists, and the constitution text itself are preserved in Norwegian national archives.

events.title: Swedish-Norwegian War and Union
events.description: After Norway declared independence and adopted its constitution, Sweden invaded on 26 July under Crown Prince Carl Johan (Bernadotte). The brief campaign lasted about two weeks; Swedish forces were vastly superior and Norwegian resistance, though spirited at several engagements, could not withstand the invasion. The Convention of Moss on 14 August ended hostilities. Christian Frederick agreed to abdicate, and the Norwegian Storting accepted a personal union with Sweden under the Swedish king, but crucially retained its constitution with only minor amendments. This compromise preserved Norwegian domestic self-governance within the union, which lasted until 1905.
key_figures: Carl Johan / Bernadotte (Swedish Crown Prince and commander)

sources.name: Convention of Moss, 14 August 1814 (treaty text)
Barton, Sweden and Visions of Norway (2003)

events.certainty_note: Contemporary military records, diplomatic correspondence, and treaty texts are fully preserved.

events.title: Allied invasion of France and the Battle of Paris
events.description: Following their victory at the Battle of Leipzig in October 1813, the armies of the Sixth Coalition crossed the Rhine into France in late 1813 and early 1814. Despite Napoleon's brilliant defensive campaign in February–March 1814 (the Six Days' Campaign), which won several tactical victories against numerically superior forces, the Allies bypassed him and marched on Paris. The Battle of Paris on 30 March 1814 saw approximately 100,000 Allied troops assault the capital's defenses, which were held by Marshals Marmont and Mortier with about 30,000 men and National Guard. After fierce fighting that cost several thousand casualties on each side, Marmont negotiated a capitulation. Paris fell on 31 March — the first time a foreign army had entered the city since 1415.
key_figures: Karl Philipp, Prince of Schwarzenberg (Allied commander)
Auguste de Marmont (Marshal of France, defended Paris)
Gebhard Leberecht von Blücher (Prussian Field Marshal)

sources.name: Correspondance de Napoléon Ier (published imperial correspondence)
Michael V. Leggiere, The Fall of Napoleon: The Allied Invasion of France, 1813–1814 (2007)
David Chandler, The Campaigns of Napoleon (1966)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in military archives, memoirs, and official dispatches of all combatant powers.

events.title: Napoleon abdicates and is exiled to Elba
events.description: With Paris fallen and his marshals refusing to continue the fight, Napoleon abdicated conditionally on 4 April and unconditionally on 6 April 1814 at Fontainebleau. The Treaty of Fontainebleau (11 April) granted him sovereignty over the island of Elba in the Mediterranean, an annual pension of 2 million francs from France, and allowed him to retain the title of Emperor. He departed for Elba on 20 April after an emotional farewell to the Old Guard at Fontainebleau. Napoleon's exile proved temporary — he would escape in February 1815, launching the Hundred Days — but the first abdication marked the end of over a decade of French imperial domination of Europe.
key_figures: Alexander I (Tsar of Russia, key Allied negotiator)

sources.name: Treaty of Fontainebleau, 11 April 1814 (treaty text)
Caulaincourt, Mémoires du Duc de Vicence (memoirs of Napoleon's chief negotiator)
Andrew Roberts, Napoleon: A Life (2014)

events.certainty_note: Documented by treaty text, extensive correspondence, and memoirs of multiple participants.

events.title: Restoration of the Bourbon monarchy under Louis XVIII
events.description: Following Napoleon's abdication, the French Senate, guided by Talleyrand, invited Louis XVIII, brother of the executed Louis XVI, to assume the throne. Louis entered Paris on 3 May 1814. He issued the Charter of 1814 (Charte constitutionnelle), which established a constitutional monarchy with a bicameral legislature, guaranteed civil liberties, confirmed the Napoleonic Code and property transfers of the Revolution, and established religious toleration while naming Catholicism the state religion. However, the returned Bourbon court's reactionary tendencies and the alienation of Napoleonic veterans created deep political tensions that would contribute to Napoleon's return in 1815.
key_figures: Charles-Maurice de Talleyrand (orchestrator of the Restoration)
Artois / future Charles X (Louis XVIII's ultra-royalist brother)

sources.name: Charte constitutionnelle du 4 juin 1814 (text of the Charter)
Philip Mansel, Louis XVIII (1981; revised 2005)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in official records, the Moniteur universel, and diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: Treaty of Chaumont — Allied unity formalized
events.description: Signed on 1 March 1814, the Treaty of Chaumont bound Britain, Russia, Austria, and Prussia into a formal 20-year alliance against France. Each power pledged 150,000 troops and agreed not to make a separate peace with Napoleon. The treaty was the critical diplomatic achievement of the Sixth Coalition, overcoming repeated divisions that had undermined earlier coalitions. Largely the work of British Foreign Secretary Castlereagh, who travelled to the Allied headquarters to prevent a negotiated peace that would leave Napoleon in power, the treaty laid the foundation for the Concert of Europe and the post-war alliance system.
key_figures: Robert Stewart, Viscount Castlereagh (British Foreign Secretary)

sources.name: Treaty of Chaumont, 1 March 1814 (treaty text)
John Bew, Castlereagh: A Life (2012)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and extensive diplomatic correspondence preserved in national archives of all signatory powers.

events.title: First Treaty of Paris
events.description: Signed on 30 May 1814, the First Treaty of Paris ended the war between France and the Sixth Coalition. The terms were remarkably lenient: France was reduced to its 1792 boundaries (slightly larger than its pre-revolutionary borders), retained most of its colonial possessions, paid no war indemnity, and was not subjected to military occupation. Art treasures looted during the Napoleonic campaigns were not required to be returned. The Allies deliberately sought to stabilize the Bourbon restoration and avoid humiliating France. The treaty also called for the convening of a general congress at Vienna to settle the broader reorganization of Europe.

sources.name: Treaty of Paris, 30 May 1814 (treaty text)
Mark Jarrett, The Congress of Vienna and Its Legacy (2013)

events.certainty_note: Full treaty text and ratification documents preserved in archives of all signatory powers.

events.title: Congress of Vienna opens
events.description: The Congress of Vienna formally opened in September 1814 (with preliminary consultations from June) and would continue until June 1815. It was the largest diplomatic gathering in European history to that date, with representatives from virtually every European state. The major powers — Britain, Russia, Austria, and Prussia — dominated proceedings, with Talleyrand skilfully inserting France into their deliberations. The Congress aimed to redraw the map of Europe after the Napoleonic upheaval, restore legitimate dynasties, and establish a stable balance of power. Its decisions would shape European geopolitics for the next century.
key_figures: Metternich (Austrian Foreign Minister, host and president)

sources.name: Protocols and Actes of the Congress of Vienna (Acte du Congrès de Vienne)
Brian Vick, The Congress of Vienna: Power and Politics after Napoleon (2014)
Adam Zamoyski, Rites of Peace: The Fall of Napoleon and the Congress of Vienna (2007)

events.certainty_note: Voluminous contemporary documentation including diplomatic correspondence, protocols, and published memoirs.

events.title: Ferdinand VII restored; abolishes Spanish Constitution
events.description: After Napoleon's fall, Ferdinand VII returned to Spain in March 1814 from his captivity in France. Rather than accept the liberal Constitution of 1812 (drafted by the Cortes of Cádiz during the Peninsular War), Ferdinand issued a decree on 4 May abolishing the constitution and dissolving the Cortes, restoring absolute monarchy. Liberal leaders were arrested and imprisoned. This reactionary restoration provoked deep political divisions in Spain that would fuel civil wars and military coups for decades. Ferdinand's rejection of constitutionalism also undermined Spanish authority in the Americas, where colonial liberals and independence leaders had looked to the 1812 constitution as a framework for reform.
key_figures: Francisco de Copons (Captain-General who supported the King's return)

sources.name: Royal decree of 4 May 1814 (Valencia Manifesto)
Charles Esdaile, Spain in the Liberal Age (2000)

events.certainty_note: Ferdinand's decrees and the political events are thoroughly documented in Spanish parliamentary and royal archives.

events.title: British forces burn Washington, D.C.
events.description: On 24 August 1814, a British expeditionary force under Major General Robert Ross defeated American militia at the Battle of Bladensburg and entered Washington, D.C. British troops burned the White House, the Capitol, the Treasury, and other government buildings in retaliation for the American burning of York (Toronto) in 1813. President Madison and the government fled the city. The burning of Washington was the most dramatic British success of the War of 1812 and remains the only time since the American Revolution that a foreign power has captured and occupied the U.S. capital. The psychological impact was enormous, though it had limited strategic effect.
key_figures: Dolley Madison (First Lady, rescued Washington's portrait)

sources.name: Anthony S. Pitch, The Burning of Washington: The British Invasion of 1814 (1998)
Contemporary dispatches of General Ross and Admiral Cockburn to the Admiralty
National Intelligencer and other American newspaper accounts, August–September 1814

events.certainty_note: Abundantly documented in both British and American military records, newspapers, and personal accounts.

events.title: Battle of Baltimore and 'The Star-Spangled Banner'
events.description: Following the burning of Washington, British forces advanced on Baltimore in September 1814. The attack on Fort McHenry on 13–14 September — a 25-hour naval bombardment — failed to reduce the fort, and the British land assault was repulsed after General Ross was killed at the Battle of North Point on 12 September. The successful American defense of Baltimore was a turning point in morale during the War of 1812. Francis Scott Key, a lawyer who witnessed the bombardment from a British ship, wrote the poem 'Defence of Fort M'Henry,' which was soon set to music and eventually became the U.S. national anthem as 'The Star-Spangled Banner' in 1931.
key_figures: Robert Ross (British general, killed at North Point)
Francis Scott Key (author of 'The Star-Spangled Banner')

sources.name: Francis Scott Key, 'Defence of Fort M'Henry' (broadside, September 1814)
Scott S. Sheads, The Rockets' Red Glare: The Maritime Defense of Baltimore in 1814 (1986)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in military records, Key's own account, and contemporary press coverage.

events.title: Treaty of Ghent ends the War of 1812
events.description: Signed on 24 December 1814, the Treaty of Ghent ended the War of 1812 between the United States and Britain. The treaty essentially restored the status quo ante bellum — no territory changed hands, and the issues of impressment and neutral trading rights that had ostensibly caused the war were not addressed. Boundary commissions were established to resolve border disputes. The treaty was ratified by the U.S. Senate in February 1815. Ironically, the Battle of New Orleans (January 1815) — Andrew Jackson's famous victory — was fought before news of the peace reached the combatants. The war's end inaugurated a lasting peace between the U.S. and Britain.

sources.name: Treaty of Ghent, 24 December 1814 (treaty text)
J.C.A. Stagg, The War of 1812: Conflict for a Continent (2012)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text fully preserved; extensive diplomatic correspondence from both delegations survives.

events.title: Anglo-Nepalese (Gurkha) War begins
events.description: In late 1814, the British East India Company declared war on the Kingdom of Nepal after escalating border disputes in the Terai region. The Gurkha kingdom under the rule of Mukhtiyar (Prime Minister) Bhimsen Thapa had been expanding aggressively along the Himalayan frontier, bringing it into conflict with British-controlled territories. The initial British campaign, launched in November 1814, went poorly — multiple British columns were repulsed with heavy losses, and the Gurkhas proved formidable mountain fighters. The war, which would continue until 1816, ultimately resulted in the Treaty of Sugauli and the beginning of the famous Gurkha recruitment into the British Indian Army.
key_figures: Francis Rawdon-Hastings, Marquess of Hastings (Governor-General of India)
Amar Singh Thapa (Gurkha commander in the west)

sources.name: John Pemble, The Invasion of Nepal: John Company at War (1971)
East India Company military records and dispatches, 1814–1816

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in East India Company records and Nepalese court histories.

events.title: Cape Colony formally ceded to Britain
events.description: The Dutch Cape Colony, which Britain had occupied militarily since 1806, was formally ceded to Britain as part of the broader post-Napoleonic settlement. The Anglo-Dutch treaty of 13 August 1814 (formalized within the Convention of London) transferred the Cape along with other Dutch possessions including parts of Guyana, in exchange for the return of the Dutch East Indies (modern Indonesia) to the Netherlands and a payment of £6 million. This transfer made British control of the Cape permanent, setting the stage for the complex colonial history of South Africa, including the displacement of the Boer population and the eventual Great Trek of the 1830s.
key_figures: Lord Charles Somerset (incoming British Governor, arrived 1814)

sources.name: Convention of London (Anglo-Dutch Treaty), 13 August 1814
Robert Ross, A Concise History of South Africa (2008)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and colonial administrative records are fully preserved.

events.title: Restoration of Pope Pius VII and the Jesuit order
events.description: Pope Pius VII, who had been imprisoned by Napoleon since 1809, was released and returned to Rome on 24 May 1814, restoring papal temporal authority over the Papal States. On 7 August, Pius issued the bull Sollicitudo omnium ecclesiarum, restoring the Society of Jesus (Jesuits) worldwide. The Jesuit order had been suppressed by Pope Clement XIV in 1773 under pressure from Bourbon monarchies. The restoration reflected the broader conservative-restorationist spirit of post-Napoleonic Europe and revived one of the most influential religious orders in history, which would go on to play a major role in Catholic education, missions, and intellectual life.
key_figures: Tadeusz Brzozowski (first Superior General of the restored Jesuits)

sources.name: Sollicitudo omnium ecclesiarum, papal bull of 7 August 1814
E.E.Y. Hales, The Emperor and the Pope: The Story of Napoleon and Pius VII (1961)

events.certainty_note: Papal documents, diplomatic correspondence, and Vatican archives provide comprehensive documentation.

events.title: George Stephenson builds his first locomotive
events.description: George Stephenson built his first steam locomotive, the Blücher, at Killingworth Colliery in July 1814. The locomotive, designed for hauling coal wagons along the colliery railway, could pull 30 tons of coal uphill at 4 mph. While not the first steam locomotive (Richard Trevithick had demonstrated one in 1804, and others had followed), Stephenson's design was the first to be commercially practical for sustained use. He continued to refine his designs over the following years, leading to the Locomotion No. 1 (1825) and the Rocket (1829), which would launch the railway age and transform global transportation and industrialization.

sources.name: Samuel Smiles, The Life of George Stephenson, Railway Engineer (1857)
Robert Stephenson and Co., company records
Simon Garfield, The Last Journey of William Huskisson (2002) — modern contextual account

events.certainty_note: Stephenson's early locomotives are well-documented in colliery records and contemporary accounts, though some details rely on Smiles' later biography.

events.title: Royalist reconquest of Venezuela — fall of the Second Republic
events.description: The Venezuelan Second Republic, established by Simón Bolívar in 1813, collapsed in 1814 under the onslaught of royalist forces led by José Tomás Boves. Boves mobilized the llaneros (plainsmen), many of them mixed-race and Black cowboys, who saw the republican cause as a Creole elite movement. The royalist victories at La Puerta (June and October 1814) were decisive. Boves's forces were notorious for extreme violence against republican populations. Bolívar was forced to flee eastward and eventually left Venezuela entirely, going into exile in Jamaica and Haiti. The fall of the Second Republic demonstrated that independence required broader social mobilization beyond the Creole elite.

sources.name: John Lynch, Simón Bolívar: A Life (2006)
Bolívar's correspondence and proclamations, 1814

events.certainty_note: Well-documented through military records, Bolívar's extensive correspondence, and royalist dispatches.

events.title: Creek War ends — Treaty of Fort Jackson
events.description: The Creek War, fought between the United States (allied with Lower Creek factions) and the Red Stick faction of the Upper Creek, ended with the Treaty of Fort Jackson on 9 August 1814. Following Andrew Jackson's decisive victory at the Battle of Horseshoe Bend (27 March 1814), in which approximately 800 Red Stick warriors were killed, the treaty forced the Creek Nation to cede roughly 23 million acres (half their territory) in present-day Alabama and Georgia. Remarkably, Jackson imposed the punitive cession on all Creek, including those who had fought alongside the Americans. The cession opened vast lands for cotton cultivation and American settlement in the Deep South.
key_figures: Menawa (Red Stick Creek leader, wounded at Horseshoe Bend)
William Weatherford / Red Eagle (Red Stick leader who surrendered)

sources.name: Treaty of Fort Jackson, 9 August 1814 (treaty text)
Gregory A. Waselkov, A Conquering Spirit: Fort Mims and the Redstick War of 1813–1814 (2006)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text, military records, and Jackson's correspondence are extensively preserved in U.S. National Archives.

events.title: Restoration of Chile's royalist control — Battle of Rancagua
events.description: The Chilean independence movement (the Patria Vieja period) ended with the Battle of Rancagua on 1–2 October 1814, when a royalist army from Peru under Mariano Osorio defeated the patriot forces under Bernardo O'Higgins. After two days of fierce fighting in the town, O'Higgins led a desperate cavalry charge to break through royalist lines and escape with survivors across the Andes to Mendoza, Argentina. The royalist restoration that followed reimposed colonial authority, persecuted patriot sympathizers, and reversed liberal reforms. The defeat drove Chilean and Argentine independence movements to coordinate, eventually leading to José de San Martín's Army of the Andes crossing in 1817.

sources.name: Simon Collier, Ideas and Politics of Chilean Independence 1808–1833 (1967)
Contemporary dispatches of the Viceroy of Peru and royalist commanders

events.certainty_note: Well-documented in Chilean and Peruvian colonial archives and patriot memoirs.

events.title: Dutch Republic reconstituted as the Kingdom of the Netherlands
events.description: Following the collapse of French control over the Low Countries, William Frederick of Orange-Nassau was proclaimed Sovereign Prince of the Netherlands in December 1813 and assumed power in early 1814. On 29 March 1814, he promulgated a constitution (Grondwet) establishing a constitutional monarchy. The Allied powers at the Congress of Vienna decided to merge the former Dutch Republic with the Austrian Netherlands (Belgium) and Luxembourg into an enlarged Kingdom of the Netherlands as a buffer state against France. William was inaugurated as King William I on 16 March 1815. This forced union of the predominantly Protestant, Dutch-speaking north with the Catholic, French- and Flemish-speaking south would prove unstable, leading to the Belgian Revolution of 1830.
key_figures: Gijsbert Karel van Hogendorp (architect of the Dutch constitution)

sources.name: Grondwet voor de Vereenigde Nederlanden, 29 March 1814 (constitutional text)
J.C.H. Blom and E. Lamberts, History of the Low Countries (2006)

events.certainty_note: Constitutional text and associated political proceedings are fully preserved in Dutch archives.

events.title: British acquisition of Malta confirmed
events.description: The First Treaty of Paris (30 May 1814) formally confirmed British sovereignty over Malta, which Britain had occupied since 1800 after the French garrison surrendered. Malta had been a possession of the Knights of St. John for centuries before Napoleon seized it in 1798. The island's strategic location in the central Mediterranean made it invaluable for British naval power, and it would serve as a key base for the Royal Navy until Maltese independence in 1964. The treaty formalized what had been a de facto military occupation into permanent colonial possession.

sources.name: Treaty of Paris, 30 May 1814, Article VII
Carmel Cassar, A Concise History of Malta (2000)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text provides explicit documentation.

events.title: Qajar Iran — end of second Russo-Persian War (Treaty of Gulistan aftermath)
events.description: The Treaty of Gulistan, signed in October 1813, was being implemented throughout 1814. Iran under the Qajar dynasty (Fath-Ali Shah) ceded most of the eastern Caucasus — including present-day Georgia, Dagestan, and parts of modern Azerbaijan — to the Russian Empire. The treaty also granted Russia exclusive rights to maintain a navy on the Caspian Sea. The loss was deeply humiliating for the Qajar state, which had expected British support that never materialized. Crown Prince Abbas Mirza began modernizing the Persian military with European advisors, preparing for what he saw as an inevitable second round of conflict, which came in 1826.
key_figures: Abbas Mirza (Crown Prince, governor of Azerbaijan province)

sources.name: Treaty of Gulistan, 24 October 1813 (treaty text, implementation 1814)
Muriel Atkin, Russia and Iran, 1780–1828 (1980)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text and Russian-Persian diplomatic correspondence are well-preserved in Russian and Iranian archives.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly cited aspects of 1814 deserve qualification. First, the burning of Washington is sometimes portrayed as pure British aggression, but British commanders explicitly cited it as retaliation for the American burning of York (Toronto) and Newark (Niagara-on-the-Lake) in 1813; the destruction was deliberately targeted at government buildings rather than being a general sacking. Second, the Treaty of Ghent is often described as a 'status quo ante bellum' peace, but some historians (notably Donald Hickey) argue that the war's outcome significantly favored Britain by ending American ambitions to annex Canada and by devastating Native American confederacies allied with Britain, whose interests were abandoned in the peace. Third, the 'leniency' of the First Treaty of Paris is sometimes overstated — while territorial terms were mild, the Allies' real leverage was the implicit threat of reimposing harsher terms, which they did after Napoleon's return in 1815 with the Second Treaty of Paris. Fourth, Stephenson's Blücher locomotive is sometimes erroneously called the 'first' locomotive; Trevithick's 1804 design, Blenkinsop's rack-rail locomotive (1812), and Hedley's Puffing Billy (1813–14) all preceded it. Stephenson's innovation was in sustained practical viability, not priority of invention.
historiographic_note: The year 1814 is one of the most extensively documented in modern history, with voluminous diplomatic archives, military records, memoirs, and newspaper coverage from all major European powers, the United States, and Latin American independence movements. The dominant sources are European diplomatic and military records, which produce a heavily Eurocentric narrative. The War of 1812 is well-documented from both American and British perspectives, though Native American viewpoints are largely reconstructed from colonial records. South American events are documented primarily through elite Creole sources, royalist dispatches, and later nationalist historiography, each with distinct biases. For regions outside the European and American spheres — Sub-Saharan Africa, inner Asia, the Pacific — the documentary record is sparse and filtered almost entirely through European observers. The Napoleonic-era sources are shaped by the memoirs of participants (many self-serving), court-sponsored histories, and the massive retrospective industry of Napoleonic historiography, which can distort the significance of events relative to non-European developments.

graph_edges.note: Norway's rejection of the Treaty of Kiel led directly to the Eidsvoll constitutional assembly
Norwegian declaration of independence provoked the Swedish military campaign
The fall of Paris forced Napoleon's abdication
Napoleon's abdication enabled the Bourbon restoration
Allied unity under the Treaty of Chaumont enabled the coordinated invasion of France
Napoleon's fall led to the peace settlement at Paris
The Treaty of Paris called for the Congress of Vienna to settle Europe
After burning Washington, British forces advanced on Baltimore
British setback at Baltimore and diplomatic negotiations at Ghent proceeded in parallel
Royalist reconquest of Venezuela and Chile were part of the same Spanish counter-revolutionary wave in 1814
Napoleon's fall freed Ferdinand VII to return to Spain from French captivity
The Cape Colony cession was part of the broader post-Napoleonic territorial settlement
The Creek War was interlinked with the War of 1812 — British support for Creek resistance
The creation of the Kingdom of the Netherlands was decided at the Congress of Vienna
